description: A diagram illustrating the high-level concept of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), showing how human feedback is used as a reward signal to optimize a language model beyond simple next-token prediction loss.
teaching_value: Helps students understand why RLHF was developed as a solution to the limitations of cross-entropy loss and rule-based metrics like BLEU/ROUGE, by visually connecting human preference signals to model optimization.
when_to_show: Show at the start of an RLHF lesson when introducing why standard loss functions and metrics like BLEU/ROUGE are insufficient for capturing human preferences in language generation.
suggested_caption: RLHF uses human feedback as a reward signal to optimize language models. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A diagram of the language model pretraining stage in the RLHF pipeline, showing how a base language model is trained before being fine-tuned with human feedback, serving as the starting point for reward model data generation.
teaching_value: Clarifies the first stage of the RLHF pipeline — pretraining — and establishes that a pretrained LM is a prerequisite before reward modeling begins, helping students understand the sequential nature of the RLHF workflow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the RLHF pipeline stages, specifically before discussing reward model training, to ground students in the prerequisite step of having a pretrained base language model.
suggested_caption: Pretraining produces the base LM used to generate reward model training data. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A diagram of the reward model training stage in the RLHF pipeline, illustrating how a preference/reward model is trained to assign scalar scores to text outputs, with context on model size choices used by OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepMind.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how human preferences are encoded into a scalar reward signal via a trained model, and grounds abstract concepts with real-world implementation details (model sizes from major labs), helping students understand design trade-offs in RLHF systems.
when_to_show: Show after explaining pretraining and before introducing the RL fine-tuning stage, when students need to understand how human preferences are operationalized as a numeric reward for policy optimization.
suggested_caption: A reward model assigns scalar scores to text, encoding human preferences for RL training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental map of the entire RLHF pipeline before diving into details, showing how each phase builds on the previous one and where RLHF fits relative to pretraining and SFT
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)

description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of token-by-token language model completion concrete and intuitive, helping students viscerally understand how LLMs generate text before learning how RLHF modifies that behavior
when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
suggested_caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens
teaching_value: Concretizes the scale of pretraining data (1T tokens) and illustrates the diversity of data sources used, helping students understand the data bottleneck problem and why data sourcing decisions matter for model behavior
when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
suggested_caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)

alt: We're at the risk of running out of Internet data
description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the looming data scarcity problem for LLM pretraining, motivating why data efficiency and alternative training signals (like human feedback) are increasingly important research directions
when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
suggested_caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)

description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT
teaching_value: Concretely shows why raw pretraining completion is insufficient for user-facing applications — the model may produce valid but unhelpful completions — making the need for SFT immediately intuitive
when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
suggested_caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields
teaching_value: Illustrates that human feedback quality depends on who the labelers are, prompting students to think critically about whose values and preferences are encoded in RLHF-trained models and the subjectivity of 'good' responses
when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
suggested_caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases
teaching_value: Clarifies the architectural and conceptual shift from imitation learning in SFT to reward-maximization in RLHF, helping students understand why a reward model is needed and how the training loop changes
when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
suggested_caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective
teaching_value: Translates the mathematical loss function into an intuitive visual, helping students understand that the RM is trained to maximize the score gap between preferred and rejected responses rather than predict absolute scores
when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
suggested_caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface
teaching_value: Makes the abstract data collection process tangible by showing the real tool used; helps students understand the practical mechanics of comparison data collection, the dual rating+ranking design, and the inter-labeler agreement challenge
when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
suggested_caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental map of the entire RLHF pipeline before diving into details, making it easier to understand where each component fits and why the KL divergence constraint is needed in phase 3.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)

description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing it as the sum of two terms: the expected reward from the reward model and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy from straying too far from the SFT model.
teaching_value: Formalizes the intuition behind RLHF optimization — students can see exactly how the reward signal and the KL divergence regularization are balanced, bridging verbal explanation to mathematical notation used in the InstructGPT paper.
when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the conceptual motivation for the KL constraint; use to transition students from intuition to formal understanding before discussing the InstructGPT paper's notation.
suggested_caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT model. (huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.
teaching_value: Illustrates the theoretical motivation for using RLHF to address hallucination, setting up the important contrast with empirical findings that RLHF actually worsened hallucination — teaching students to distinguish theoretical goals from observed outcomes.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the hallucination section, before presenting the InstructGPT empirical results, to create a productive tension between Schulman's hypothesis and the actual findings.
suggested_caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based solutions to LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)

description: A chart or figure from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit worse hallucination rates compared to SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.
teaching_value: Provides concrete empirical evidence that RLHF has trade-offs — improving instruction-following and overall preference while degrading factual accuracy — teaching students to critically evaluate model improvements rather than assuming they are uniformly beneficial.
when_to_show: Show immediately after Schulman's proposed fix (image 3) to highlight the gap between theory and practice, and use to spark discussion about evaluation trade-offs in LLM alignment.
suggested_caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF worsens hallucination despite improving overall human preference. (huyenchip.com)

description: A two-branch diagram showing two complementary approaches to achieving high data quality: one branch focused on improving rater quality (e.g., better annotators, training, guidelines) and another on aggregation/modeling techniques (e.g., majority vote, weighted aggregation, learned models).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire solution space for data quality, helping them categorize specific techniques they will encounter later in the article into either the 'human/process' or 'algorithmic/modeling' bucket.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on human data quality to orient students before diving into specific methods; use as a running reference to locate each new technique within the overall framework.
suggested_caption: Two complementary strategies for high-quality human annotation data. (Weng 2024)

description: A structured diagram illustrating the quality assurance process for crowdsourced annotation, showing how quality attributes feed into a quality model, which in turn drives quality assurance actions that loop back to improve the annotation process.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students that quality assurance is a feedback loop — not a one-time check — and maps abstract concepts like 'quality model' and 'quality attributes' onto a process flow they can apply to real annotation pipelines.
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing how to operationalize data quality standards; useful before explaining specific QA mechanisms like inter-annotator agreement or rater audits.
suggested_caption: Quality assurance as a feedback loop over annotation quality attributes. (Daniel et al. 2018)

description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Bar chart comparing expert-expert vs. expert-non-expert agreement rates on MT ranking tasks under different weighting schemes (uniform, weighted by experts, weighted by non-experts), with a 1/3 chance baseline marked. (Right) Bar chart comparing BLEU scores of translations from AMT non-experts vs. LCD professional translators across multiple MT systems.
teaching_value: Empirically demonstrates that non-expert crowd workers can approach expert-level agreement when spammers are down-weighted, and that crowd-sourced reference translations are competitive with professional ones — directly supporting the 'wisdom of the crowd' thesis with quantitative evidence.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the wisdom-of-the-crowd concept and when discussing how to handle low-quality annotators; pairs well with explaining weighting schemes for aggregating noisy labels.
suggested_caption: Non-expert AMT workers match experts in MT evaluation when spammers are down-weighted. (Callison-Burch 2009)

description: A heatmap or scatter/bar chart showing Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients between non-expert crowdworker annotations and expert (Trust & Safety professional) annotations, broken down by conversation topic, revealing high variance across topics.
teaching_value: Illustrates that annotator agreement is not uniform — it depends heavily on topic — teaching students that a single aggregate agreement metric can mask important topic-level disparities and motivating stratified evaluation.
when_to_show: Use when discussing sources of rater disagreement and the descriptive annotation paradigm; effective for motivating why demographic and topic metadata should be collected alongside labels.
suggested_caption: Expert–non-expert annotation agreement varies substantially across conversation topics. (Wang et al. 2023)

description: A hierarchical taxonomy tree diagram categorizing causes of rater disagreement into major branches such as task/item difficulty, annotator characteristics (identity, knowledge, values), and stochastic/random errors, with leaf-level examples for each.
teaching_value: Provides a structured vocabulary for diagnosing why annotators disagree, helping students distinguish between disagreement that should be preserved (genuine perspective differences) vs. disagreement that should be corrected (random errors, misunderstandings).
when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the rater disagreement section; revisit when explaining disagreement deconvolution and the descriptive vs. prescriptive annotation paradigms.
suggested_caption: Taxonomy of root causes for rater disagreement in human annotation. (Zhang et al. 2023)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing four model setups for handling multi-annotator labels: (1) single model trained on majority vote, (2) single model trained on individual labels, (3) multi-task model with one head per annotator sharing a common encoder, and (4) a variant thereof — all built on a shared BERT backbone.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts how different modeling choices encode or discard annotator identity information, making it clear why the multi-task approach can capture systematic disagreement that majority-vote baselines erase.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how to model annotator disagreement computationally; use to motivate the multi-task architecture before presenting its F1 and uncertainty estimation results.
suggested_caption: Four architectures for learning from multi-annotator labels on a shared BERT encoder. (Davani et al. 2021)

description: A conceptual illustration of the jury learning framework: individual annotators with demographic characteristics are treated as 'jurors'; a practitioner specifies a jury composition; the model samples predictions from each juror and aggregates them into a final verdict.
teaching_value: Makes the analogy between legal jury selection and ML label aggregation concrete, helping students understand how jury learning allows practitioners to deliberately control whose perspectives are represented in a model's decisions.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from passive disagreement modeling to active jury composition; effective for discussions on fairness and whose values a model should reflect.
suggested_caption: Jury learning aggregates predictions from demographically specified annotator 'jurors.' (Gordon et al. 2022)

description: A detailed neural network architecture diagram of the Deep & Cross Network (DCN) used for jury learning, showing parallel input streams for comment text (processed by a pre-trained language model), annotator embedding, and group/demographic embedding, which are combined through cross and deep layers before a final prediction head.
teaching_value: Shows students exactly how annotator identity and demographic group information are encoded and fused with text representations in a concrete model architecture, bridging the conceptual jury learning idea to an implementable design.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the jury learning concept when students need to understand the technical implementation; useful for students with a deep learning background who want architectural details.
suggested_caption: DCN architecture jointly embeds comment text, annotator identity, and demographics for jury learning. (Gordon et al. 2022)

description: Bar or line charts comparing jury learning against an annotator-agnostic baseline across multiple jury compositions (e.g., all-male, all-female, balanced, random), showing differences in predicted toxicity rates or F1 scores, demonstrating that jury composition meaningfully shifts model outputs.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that jury composition matters — the same content receives different labels depending on who is in the jury — reinforcing why the choice of annotator pool is a consequential design decision, not a neutral one.
when_to_show: Present after explaining the jury learning architecture to show its practical impact; effective for motivating discussions on fairness and representation in annotation pipelines.
suggested_caption: Jury composition significantly shifts model predictions compared to an annotator-agnostic baseline. (Gordon et al. 2022)

description: A scatter plot comparing influence function estimates of the effect of removing each training point against actual leave-one-out retraining results on 10-class MNIST, showing strong linear correlation and validating the closed-form approximation.
teaching_value: Validates the influence function approximation empirically, teaching students that the mathematically derived closed form is practically reliable for identifying harmful or helpful training examples without expensive retraining.
when_to_show: Show when introducing influence functions as a data quality tool; use to justify why the approximation is trustworthy before discussing its application to detecting mislabeled or low-quality training examples.
suggested_caption: Influence function estimates closely match leave-one-out retraining on MNIST. (Kohn & Liang 2017)

description: A 2D scatter plot (data map) of the SNLI training set using a RoBERTa classifier, with axes for 'variability' (x-axis) and 'confidence' (y-axis). Points are colored by region: easy-to-learn (high confidence, low variability), ambiguous (high variability), and hard-to-learn (low confidence, low variability). Density contours show clustering of samples in each region.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how training dynamics (confidence and variability across epochs) can partition a dataset into qualitatively different regions, helping students understand that not all training samples are equally informative or reliable — hard-to-learn samples cluster distinctly and may indicate mislabeling.
when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the Data Maps method (Swayamdipta et al. 2020) — show it to anchor the abstract concepts of 'confidence' and 'variability' to a concrete visual partition of a real dataset before discussing how these regions relate to data quality.
suggested_caption: Data map of SNLI training set partitioned by confidence and variability. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)

description: A side-by-side or overlaid data map scatter plot showing how data points shift in the confidence-variability space after their labels are artificially flipped. Originally high-confidence, low-variability (easy-to-learn) points migrate toward low-confidence, slightly higher-variability regions after label flipping, visually confirming that mislabeled samples appear in the hard-to-learn region.
teaching_value: Provides causal evidence for the hypothesis that mislabeled samples occupy the hard-to-learn region of a data map. By showing the before/after effect of deliberate label corruption, it helps students understand why low confidence + low variability is a signal for label noise rather than just sample difficulty.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the data map figure (Image 1) when explaining how the hard-to-learn region relates to mislabeling — use it as empirical validation of the Data Maps hypothesis before moving to other detection methods.
suggested_caption: Label-flipped points shift to low-confidence regions, validating data map mislabel detection. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)

description: A diagram or plot illustrating how artificially inserted 'threshold samples' (with known incorrect labels) are used in the Area Under the Margin (AUM) method to set a decision boundary separating mislabeled from correctly labeled samples. Shows AUM score distributions for clean, mislabeled, and threshold samples, demonstrating that threshold samples help calibrate the cutoff.
teaching_value: Clarifies the clever mechanism of using synthetic threshold samples to avoid needing a hand-tuned threshold for AUM-based mislabel detection. Students can see how the known AUM of fake mislabeled samples provides a principled, data-driven threshold — a key algorithmic insight.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the AUM method and the threshold-sample trick — show it after defining the margin formula to make the threshold-setting mechanism concrete before presenting experimental results.
suggested_caption: Threshold samples calibrate AUM cutoff to separate mislabeled from clean data. (Pleiss et al. 2020)

description: A bar or line chart comparing test error on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks under varying levels of randomly mislabeled training data, contrasting AUM-based data filtering against other methods such as NCV, MentorNet, and standard training with noisy labels.
teaching_value: Provides quantitative evidence for the effectiveness of AUM-based mislabel filtering relative to competing approaches. Helps students evaluate trade-offs between methods and understand that algorithmic choices for handling noisy labels have measurable downstream impact on model accuracy.
when_to_show: Show after explaining both AUM and NCV methods — use it to compare methods empirically and prompt discussion about which approach is most practical and why performance varies with noise rate.
suggested_caption: AUM filtering vs. other methods on CIFAR-10/100 with varying label noise rates. (Pleiss et al. 2020)

description: A pseudocode or flowchart of the INCV (Iterative Noisy Cross-Validation) algorithm, showing the iterative process: splitting data into halves, training on each half, identifying 'clean' samples whose labels match cross-validated predictions, removing likely noisy samples, and repeating until convergence.
teaching_value: Makes the iterative logic of INCV explicit and step-by-step, helping students understand how repeated cross-validation rounds progressively purify a noisy dataset. The algorithmic view clarifies why iteration improves over single-pass NCV and how the clean set grows over rounds.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from NCV to INCV — show it to make the iterative refinement process concrete, especially when students ask how the method handles high noise rates where a single pass is insufficient.
suggested_caption: INCV algorithm iteratively removes mislabeled samples via cross-validated label agreement. (Chen et al. 2019)
